questions.text: I process accounts of hardship primarily by considering what could be done differently rather than by dwelling on the pain itself.
My internal experience is like a symphony played at full volume — nothing is muted.
I find that the felt weight of another person's suffering is the consideration that most often tips my decision-making in a new direction.
I feel that a life full of surprises and variety is a rich life.
I feel like I have a sixth sense for when something is off in a social situation.
I find that I automatically catalog the relationships between people when I enter a new social group.
I prefer to receive tasks from a coordinator rather than assign them.
I often do not notice when a compliment carries a double meaning.
I experience the ups and downs of life as gentle undulations rather than dramatic swings.
I find that my ideas often shape the direction a group takes.
I am most productive when I have full autonomy over direction.
I make it a point to arrive early to appointments and meetings.
I believe that serving others without expectation of return is a core part of who I am.
I find that I am most engaged when working on something that directly serves my vision.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that reveals to me most plainly what it means to be morally awake in a world full of need.
I often need time alone after social events to process the intensity of my reactions.
I tend to donate or share resources before setting aside savings for myself.
I feel unsettled when I realize I've forgotten something I was supposed to do.
